text.title: Mob Programming Core Rules
text.summary: The four load-bearing rules of mob programming: strong-style navigation, rotation, written goal, and break cadence.

rule.statement: For an idea to go from your head to the computer, it must go through someone else's hands. The Driver types only what the Navigator dictates; the Driver does not make independent decisions.
rule.rationale: Without this constraint, the most fluent typist dominates and the mob collapses into one person coding with an audience. Strong-style enforces contribution from every navigator.

rule.statement: Navigate at the intent level, not the dictation level. "Create a User class storing name and email" beats "type public class User".
rule.rationale: Dictation-level navigation transfers no knowledge. Intent-level navigation requires the driver to translate understanding into code, cementing learning for everyone.

rule.statement: Rotate the driver every 5-10 minutes, enforced by a visible timer. No exceptions during the learning phase.
rule.rationale: Without rotation, driver fatigue sets in and navigators disengage. The mob becomes a lecture with one person coding. Strict rotation builds the muscle; experienced mobs can later switch to natural transitions.

rule.statement: Write a single-sentence Done definition before the session starts. Display it visibly throughout. If you cannot write it, do not start the mob.
rule.rationale: Without a written goal, mob sessions drift into exploration and the retrospective becomes "we discussed a lot" — that is procrastination, not collaboration.

rule.statement: Take a real break (away from the screen) every 75-90 minutes. Skipping breaks is the primary cause of post-mob burnout and introvert attrition.
rule.rationale: Sustained mob work is cognitively intense. Breaks allow individuals to process, recover, and return engaged. Sessions without breaks degrade in quality after 90 minutes regardless of rotation.

rule.statement: Hold a written retrospective after every session: what worked, what slowed us, one experiment for next time.
rule.rationale: The retrospective is where the mob actually learns to mob. Without it, every session repeats the same friction. In writing because verbal-only retrospectives leave no trace for the next session.
